u1_l1.1.title: Levels of Organization (Cells → Tissues → Organs → Systems)

flashcards.definition: Atoms and molecules; the simplest structural level of the body.
The basic living unit of all organisms; performs all life functions.
A group of similar cells performing a common function (4 types).
A structure composed of two or more tissue types that performs a specific function.
A group of organs that work together to accomplish a common purpose (e.g., cardiovascular system).
Standing erect, feet forward, arms at sides, palms facing anteriorly.
Divides the body into left and right portions; midsagittal = equal halves.
Divides the body into anterior (front) and posterior (back) portions.
Divides the body into superior (upper) and inferior (lower) portions.
Medial = toward the midline; lateral = away from the midline.
Maintenance of a stable internal environment (temperature, pH, blood glucose, etc.).
A response that reverses (reduces) the initial stimulus; most common feedback type.
A response that amplifies the stimulus until the process is complete.
The sensor that detects a change in the internal or external environment.
The organ/tissue that carries out the corrective response (e.g., muscle, gland).
Lines and covers surfaces; classified by cell shape and layer number.
Supports and binds structures; most abundant and diverse tissue type.
Voluntary, striated, attached to bones; responsible for movement.
Involuntary, striated, found only in the heart; self-exciting.
Involuntary, nonstriated; found in walls of hollow organs.
Ionizing radiation technique; best for bone structures and fractures.
Computed Tomography; multiple X-ray slices assembled into cross-sectional images.
Magnetic Resonance Imaging; uses magnetic fields for excellent soft-tissue detail, no radiation.
Uses sound waves; safe (no radiation), commonly used for fetal imaging.
Positron Emission Tomography; radioactive tracers reveal metabolic activity (cancer, brain).
Excess water loss triggers ADH release, increasing kidney water reabsorption.
Released by posterior pituitary; causes kidneys to reabsorb water.
Autoimmune destruction of insulin-producing beta cells → chronic hyperglycemia.
Failure of thermoregulatory homeostasis; core temp > 40°C; medical emergency.
When the body cannot maintain stable conditions; leads to disease or death.
The layer of a serous membrane that lines the wall of a body cavity.
The layer that directly covers an organ (e.g., visceral pleura covers the lung).
Serous membrane surrounding the lungs (visceral + parietal layers).
Serous membrane surrounding the heart.
Serous membrane lining the abdominopelvic cavity and covering abdominal organs.
The shaft of a long bone; composed of compact bone surrounding the medullary cavity.
The end of a long bone; contains spongy bone and articular cartilage at the joint surface.
Dense fibrous membrane covering the outer surface of bone (except at joints).
The production of blood cells, occurring in red bone marrow.
Growth plate made of cartilage; allows bones to lengthen during development.

u2_l2.2.title: Types of Bones (Long, Short, Flat, Irregular)

flashcards.definition: Longer than wide, with a shaft and two ends (e.g., femur, humerus).
Cube-shaped; found in wrist (carpals) and ankle (tarsals).
Thin, flattened, often curved; provides protection and broad surfaces (e.g., skull, sternum).
Complex, non-uniform shape (e.g., vertebrae, hip bones).
Small bone embedded within a tendon; the patella is the largest example.
80 bones forming the body's central axis: skull, vertebral column, bony thorax.
7 neck vertebrae (C1–C7); C1 = atlas, C2 = axis.
12 vertebrae (T1–T12) articulating with ribs.
5 lower back vertebrae (L1–L5); largest and strongest.
True ribs (1–7) attach directly to the sternum; false ribs (8–12) do not attach directly.
126 bones: limbs plus pectoral and pelvic girdles.
Clavicle + scapula; attaches upper limb to the axial skeleton.
Two hip bones (ilium, ischium, pubis each) + sacrum; attaches lower limb.
The single bone of the upper arm.
The thighbone; the longest and strongest bone in the body.
Most movable joint type; contains a joint cavity filled with synovial fluid.
Decreasing the angle between two body parts (e.g., bending the elbow).
Increasing the angle between two body parts (e.g., straightening the elbow).
Abduction = away from midline; adduction = toward midline.
Allows the greatest range of motion (e.g., hip, shoulder).
Bone forms directly from mesenchyme without a cartilage model (e.g., skull flat bones).
Bone replaces a hyaline cartilage template; how most bones develop.
Bone grows in width by adding layers on the outer surface via osteoblasts.
Stimulates osteoclasts to resorb bone, raising blood calcium levels.
Hormone from the thyroid that inhibits osteoclasts, lowering blood calcium.
Progressive loss of bone density → increased fracture risk; most common metabolic bone disease.
Degenerative joint disease from wear and tear on articular cartilage.
Autoimmune disorder where the immune system attacks joint linings.
Genetic collagen disorder resulting in extremely brittle, easily fractured bones.
A fracture where bone pierces through the skin; high infection risk.
A hole or opening in bone for passage of blood vessels or nerves.
A bony projection or prominence.
A shallow depression in a bone surface.
A large, rounded articular surface for joint formation.
A rough, raised area on bone for muscle or ligament attachment.
Voluntary, striated, multinucleated; moves the skeleton.
Involuntary, striated, branching; has intercalated discs; found only in the heart.
Involuntary, nonstriated, spindle-shaped; lines hollow organs.
Gap junctions between cardiac muscle cells allowing synchronized contraction.
The ability of cardiac muscle to generate its own electrical impulses.
The basic contractile unit of striated muscle; A band + I band + Z lines.
A thread-like organelle within a muscle fiber composed of repeating sarcomeres.
Specialized smooth ER in muscle that stores and releases calcium ions.
The synapse between a motor neuron and a skeletal muscle fiber.
Neurotransmitter released at the NMJ that triggers muscle contraction.
Actin (thin) filaments slide past myosin (thick) filaments, shortening the sarcomere.
Myosin heads attach to actin, pivot (power stroke), detach, re-cock — repeating cycle.
Regulatory protein on actin that binds Ca²⁺ and moves tropomyosin off the binding sites.
Protein covering actin binding sites at rest; moves when Ca²⁺ binds troponin.
The pivoting of the myosin head that pulls actin, generating force.
Muscle changes length; concentric (shortens) or eccentric (lengthens under load).
Muscle generates force without changing length (e.g., holding a wall push).
Immediate energy reserve; donates phosphate to ADP → ATP (lasts ~8 seconds).
Glucose broken down without oxygen; yields 2 ATP + lactic acid.
O₂-dependent breakdown of glucose/fat; yields ~36 ATP per glucose.
Anterior upper arm; flexes elbow and supinates forearm.
Posterior upper arm; extends the elbow.
Four muscles on the anterior thigh; extends the knee.
Three posterior thigh muscles; flex the knee and extend the hip.
Largest muscle in the body; extends and laterally rotates the hip.
Genetic disorders causing progressive muscle weakness and degeneration.
Autoimmune disease where antibodies destroy ACh receptors → muscle weakness.
Chronic condition with widespread musculoskeletal pain and fatigue.
Rapid skeletal muscle destruction releasing myoglobin; can cause kidney failure.
Injury to a muscle or tendon (overstretching/tearing).
Key knee stabilizer preventing anterior tibial translation; common sports injury.
Four muscles (SITS: supraspinatus, infraspinatus, teres minor, subscapularis) stabilizing the shoulder.
Thickest and strongest tendon; connects calf muscles to the heel bone (calcaneus).
Mild traumatic brain injury from a blow to the head; requires careful management.
Chronic Traumatic Encephalopathy; degenerative brain disease from repeated head impacts.
Activating more motor units to increase force; small units first (size principle).
Increasing stimulation frequency → increased force → tetanus.
Maximal force is generated when sarcomeres are at optimal actin-myosin overlap.
Slow-twitch, oxidative, fatigue-resistant; endurance activities.
Fast-twitch, glycolytic, fatigue quickly; powerful, rapid movements.
Functional unit of the nervous system; transmits electrical signals.
Branching extension of a neuron that receives incoming signals.
Long projection that carries nerve impulses away from the cell body.
Insulating lipid layer around axons that speeds signal conduction.
Star-shaped glial cell; supports neurons, contributes to the blood-brain barrier.
~−70 mV; maintained by Na⁺/K⁺ pump and ion channel leaks.
Na⁺ channels open; Na⁺ influx makes the interior positive.
K⁺ channels open; K⁺ efflux restores negative interior.
The membrane potential (~−55 mV) that must be reached to trigger an action potential.
Junction between two neurons where neurotransmitters transmit the signal.
Largest brain region; cerebral cortex handles thought, language, motor, sensation.
'Little brain' at the posterior; coordinates movement and balance.
Midbrain + pons + medulla oblongata; controls vital autonomic functions.
Relay center for sensory information heading to the cerebral cortex.
Controls homeostasis, body temperature, hunger, thirst, and the endocrine system.
Peripheral Nervous System; all nerves and ganglia outside the CNS.
Carry signals from sensors/receptors TO the CNS.
Carry signals FROM the CNS to muscles and glands.
Voluntary motor control of skeletal muscles.
12 pairs emerging from the brain; serve head, neck, and (vagus) thoracic/abdominal organs.
'Fight or flight'; prepares body for stress — ↑HR, ↑BP, dilated pupils, inhibited digestion.
'Rest and digest'; promotes calm functions — ↓HR, stimulated digestion, constricted pupils.
Most organs receive both sympathetic and parasympathetic signals for balanced control.
Primary neurotransmitter of the sympathetic postganglionic neurons.
Neurotransmitter used by all preganglionic neurons and parasympathetic postganglionic neurons.
Neural pathway for a reflex: receptor → sensory neuron → CNS → motor neuron → effector.
Only one synapse (sensory → motor); e.g., patellar reflex.
Multiple synapses involving interneurons; e.g., withdrawal reflex.
Knee-jerk reflex: tapping the patellar tendon stretches the quadriceps → leg kicks.
Pulling hand away from a hot surface; polysynaptic, involves interneurons.
Progressive neurodegenerative disorder; memory loss, amyloid plaques, neurofibrillary tangles.
Loss of dopaminergic neurons → tremor, rigidity, slow movement (bradykinesia).
Condition of recurrent seizures from abnormal brain electrical activity.
Cerebrovascular accident; ischemic (clot) or hemorrhagic (bleed) interrupting brain blood flow.
Inflammation of the meninges; can be caused by bacteria or viruses.
Local, decremental signals (EPSPs/IPSPs) that can summate to reach threshold.
Temporal (rapid stimuli from one source) or spatial (simultaneous stimuli from multiple sources).
~−55 mV; the critical depolarization level that triggers a full action potential.
Absolute: no AP possible. Relative: stronger-than-normal stimulus can trigger AP.
The brain's ability to reorganize connections; basis of learning, memory, and recovery.
Chemical messenger produced by endocrine glands; travels via blood to target cells.
Ductless gland that secretes hormones directly into the bloodstream.
A cell with specific receptors for a particular hormone.
Intracellular molecule (e.g., cAMP) that relays the signal from a surface receptor.
Lipid-soluble hormone derived from cholesterol; crosses membrane to bind nuclear receptors.
'Master gland'; anterior lobe produces GH, TSH, ACTH, FSH, LH, prolactin; posterior stores ADH, oxytocin.
Produces T3/T4 (regulate metabolism) and calcitonin (lowers blood calcium).
Cortex: cortisol, aldosterone. Medulla: epinephrine, norepinephrine.
β-cells secrete insulin (↓glucose); α-cells secrete glucagon (↑glucose).
Four small glands; secrete PTH which raises blood calcium levels.
Insulin (↓glucose) and glucagon (↑glucose) maintain blood sugar within a narrow range.
PTH raises Ca²⁺; calcitonin lowers Ca²⁺; vitamin D aids absorption.
Released when dehydrated; causes kidneys to reabsorb more water, concentrating urine.
Response reduces the original stimulus; primary mechanism for hormonal regulation.
Maintenance of stable internal conditions despite external changes.

u5_l5.4.title: Feedback Loops in the Endocrine System

flashcards.definition: The dominant control mechanism; the product inhibits further production to maintain a set point.
Rare; the product amplifies the stimulus (e.g., oxytocin in labor, LH surge).
Hypothalamic-Pituitary-Adrenal axis: CRH → ACTH → cortisol; cortisol inhibits CRH and ACTH.
Hypothalamic-Pituitary-Thyroid axis: TRH → TSH → T3/T4; T3/T4 inhibit TRH and TSH.
Hypothalamic-Pituitary-Gonadal axis: GnRH → FSH/LH → sex hormones; sex hormones feed back.
Chronic hyperglycemia; Type 1 (no insulin) vs. Type 2 (insulin resistance).
Autoimmune hyperthyroidism; antibodies stimulate the thyroid.
Autoimmune hypothyroidism; gradual thyroid destruction.
Chronic excess cortisol causing moon face, central obesity, and metabolic issues.
Excess GH in adults; enlarged hands, feet, and facial features.
The 'three Ps' of diabetes: excessive urination, thirst, and hunger.
Glycated hemoglobin; reflects average blood glucose over ~3 months.
Antibodies against thyroid peroxidase; marker for Hashimoto's thyroiditis.
Life-threatening cortisol deficiency; can occur with sudden steroid withdrawal.
First-line oral medication for Type 2 diabetes; reduces hepatic glucose production.
TRH → TSH → T3/T4; negative feedback from thyroid hormones.
CRH → ACTH → cortisol; stress axis; negative feedback from cortisol.
GnRH → FSH/LH → sex hormones; reproductive regulation.
A hormone that stimulates another endocrine gland (e.g., TSH, ACTH, FSH, LH).
One hormone enhances the effect of another (e.g., thyroid hormones potentiate catecholamine effects).
Pumps deoxygenated blood to the lungs via the pulmonary artery.
Pumps oxygenated blood to the body via the aorta; has the thickest wall.
AV valve between the right atrium and right ventricle (3 cusps).
AV valve between the left atrium and left ventricle (2 cusps).
The thick muscular layer of the heart wall; responsible for contraction.
Thick-walled vessel carrying blood away from the heart; high pressure.
Thinnest vessel (one endothelial cell thick); site of exchange between blood and tissues.
Thin-walled vessel carrying blood toward the heart; has valves to prevent backflow.
RV → pulmonary artery → lungs (gas exchange) → pulmonary veins → LA.
Venous blood from GI tract goes to the liver for processing before returning to the heart.
Liquid component of blood (~55%); water, proteins, electrolytes, nutrients, waste.
Red blood cell; contains hemoglobin for O₂ transport; no nucleus.
White blood cell; immune defense. Types: neutrophils, lymphocytes, monocytes, eosinophils, basophils.
Cell fragment from megakaryocytes; essential for blood clotting (hemostasis).
Iron-containing protein in RBCs that binds and transports oxygen.
Contraction phase; ventricles eject blood into arteries.
Relaxation phase; ventricles fill with blood from atria.
Sinoatrial node; the heart's natural pacemaker (sets the rhythm).
Atrioventricular node; delays the impulse so atria contract before ventricles.
Electrocardiogram; records electrical activity: P wave, QRS complex, T wave.
Peak pressure during ventricular contraction (top number); normal ~120 mmHg.
Lowest pressure during ventricular relaxation (bottom number); normal ~80 mmHg.
Pressure sensors in carotid sinus and aortic arch; detect BP changes for short-term regulation.
Renin-Angiotensin-Aldosterone System; long-term BP regulation via blood volume control.
Chronically elevated blood pressure (≥130/80); 'silent killer'; major cardiovascular risk factor.
Atherosclerosis of coronary arteries; can cause angina or heart attack (MI).
Heart attack; death of heart muscle due to blocked coronary artery.
Heart unable to pump blood effectively; fluid backs up (edema, dyspnea).
Chest pain from reduced blood flow to the myocardium (ischemia without infarction).
Atherosclerosis in peripheral arteries; causes leg pain (claudication) with walking.
ST-Elevation Myocardial Infarction; complete coronary artery blockage requiring emergent reperfusion.
Cardiac protein released when heart muscle is damaged; key biomarker for MI.
% of blood ejected per beat; normal ~55-70%. Low EF = systolic heart failure.
Percutaneous Coronary Intervention; catheter-based procedure to open blocked coronary arteries (angioplasty/stent).
Severely elevated BP with acute end-organ damage; requires immediate treatment.
Atrial depolarization; should precede every QRS in normal sinus rhythm.
Time from start of P to start of QRS (0.12-0.20 s); prolonged in heart block.
Ventricular depolarization; normally 0.06-0.10 s wide.
Between QRS and T wave; elevation or depression indicates ischemia/injury.
Irregular rhythm with no identifiable P waves; atria quiver instead of contracting.
Tiny air sacs (~300 million) in the lungs where O₂/CO₂ exchange occurs with capillaries.
Windpipe; supported by C-shaped cartilage rings; leads to the bronchi.
Voice box; contains vocal cords; epiglottis guards the airway during swallowing.
Two main branches of the trachea (right and left); carry air into each lung.
Double-layered serous membrane (visceral + parietal) surrounding each lung.
Active process; diaphragm contracts, thoracic volume increases, pressure drops, air flows in.
Passive at rest (elastic recoil); forced expiration uses internal intercostals and abdominals.
Volume of air inhaled/exhaled in a normal breath (~500 mL).
Maximum air exhaled after maximum inhalation (~4.8 L).
Air remaining in lungs after maximum exhalation (~1.2 L); prevents alveolar collapse.
The pressure exerted by a single gas in a mixture; drives diffusion.
Alveolar epithelium + capillary endothelium; ~0.5 µm; where gas exchange occurs.
Ideal ratio of alveolar airflow to capillary blood flow for efficient gas exchange.
Destructive lung disease that reduces alveolar surface area → impaired gas exchange.
Thickening/scarring of lung tissue increasing diffusion distance → impaired gas exchange.
Hemoglobin bound to O₂ (HbO₂); forms in the lungs, releases O₂ in tissues.
Sigmoidal curve showing Hb's O₂ saturation vs. PO₂; right shift = easier O₂ unloading.
Primary form of CO₂ transport in blood (~70%); formed by carbonic anhydrase in RBCs.
Enzyme in RBCs catalyzing CO₂ + H₂O ↔ H₂CO₃ ↔ H⁺ + HCO₃⁻.
Increased H⁺ or CO₂ decreases Hb's affinity for O₂ → promotes O₂ release in active tissues.
Brainstem region that generates the basic rhythm of breathing.
In the medulla; primarily sense ↑CO₂ (via H⁺ in CSF) to increase ventilation.
In carotid/aortic bodies; detect low O₂, high CO₂, low pH.
Stretch receptors in lungs prevent overinflation by inhibiting inspiration.
Normal stimulus to breathe: rising CO₂ detected by chemoreceptors → increased ventilation.
Chronic inflammatory airway disease with reversible bronchospasm; wheezing, dyspnea.
Chronic Obstructive Pulmonary Disease; chronic bronchitis + emphysema; usually from smoking.
Lung infection causing alveolar fluid/pus accumulation → impaired gas exchange.
Destruction of alveolar walls reducing surface area; irreversible; component of COPD.
Blood clot (usually from DVT) blocking a pulmonary artery; life-threatening.
Measures maximum airflow during forced exhalation; reduced in asthma/COPD.
Solid-appearing area on CXR where alveoli are filled with fluid/pus (e.g., pneumonia).
Blood-tinged sputum characteristic of Streptococcus pneumoniae pneumonia.
In COPD, O₂ is given at low flow to avoid suppressing the hypoxic drive.
pH < 7.35 with PaCO₂ > 45 mmHg; caused by hypoventilation/CO₂ retention.
P₁V₁ = P₂V₂; as volume increases, pressure decreases (and vice versa).
Total pressure of a gas mixture = sum of each gas's partial pressure.
Amount of gas dissolved in liquid is proportional to its partial pressure.
pH < 7.35, PaCO₂ > 45 mmHg; caused by hypoventilation.
pH > 7.45, PaCO₂ < 35 mmHg; caused by hyperventilation.
The continuous tube from mouth to anus through which food passes.
Wave-like smooth muscle contractions that propel food through the GI tract.
Innermost GI wall layer; responsible for secretion and absorption.
Primary site of digestion and absorption; three parts: duodenum, jejunum, ileum.
Serous membrane lining the abdominal cavity and covering most abdominal organs.
Physical breakdown (chewing, churning, segmentation) increasing surface area for enzymes.
Enzymatic breakdown of macromolecules into absorbable units (monosaccharides, amino acids, fatty acids).
Enzyme in saliva that begins starch digestion in the mouth.
Protein-digesting enzyme active in the stomach's acidic environment (pH ~2).
Produced by liver, stored in gallbladder; emulsifies fats for lipase action.
Finger-like projections in the small intestine; each contains capillaries and a lacteal for absorption.
Microscopic projections on enterocytes; vastly increase surface area for absorption.
Lipoprotein particle that transports dietary fats from enterocytes into lymphatic lacteals.
Lymphatic capillary within each villus; absorbs dietary fats (chylomicrons).
Carries nutrient-rich blood from the GI tract to the liver for processing.
Largest internal organ; bile production, detoxification, protein synthesis, storage.
Stores and concentrates bile; releases into duodenum when stimulated by CCK.
Produces bicarbonate and digestive enzymes (amylase, lipase, proteases).
Breakdown product of hemoglobin; processed by the liver; excess → jaundice.
Produce saliva containing water, amylase (starch digestion), mucin, and lysozyme.
Gastroesophageal reflux disease; chronic acid reflux damaging the esophagus.
Erosion of the stomach or duodenal lining; H. pylori and NSAIDs are common causes.
IBD that can affect any GI segment (mouth to anus); transmural inflammation.
IBD limited to the colon; superficial (mucosal) inflammation with continuous lesions.
Autoimmune disorder triggered by gluten; destroys intestinal villi → malabsorption.

u8_l8.6.title: Case Studies in Nutrition & GI Health

flashcards.definition: Storage form of iron; low ferritin = iron stores depleted.
Small, pale RBCs characteristic of iron-deficiency anemia.
Elevated serum lipase is the most specific marker for acute pancreatitis.
Anti-tissue transglutaminase; serological marker for celiac disease.
Flattening/loss of intestinal villi; seen in celiac disease on biopsy.
Stomach hormone; stimulates HCl secretion and gastric motility.
Released by duodenum in response to fat/protein; stimulates gallbladder contraction and pancreatic enzyme secretion.
Released by duodenum in response to acid; stimulates pancreatic bicarbonate secretion.
Inhibits gastric secretion and motility; stimulates insulin release.
Duodenal enzyme that activates trypsinogen → trypsin, initiating protein digestion cascade.
Functional unit of the kidney; filters blood and produces urine (~1 million per kidney).
Capillary tuft inside Bowman's capsule; site of blood filtration.
Outer region of the kidney containing glomeruli and convoluted tubules.
Inner region of the kidney containing loops of Henle and collecting ducts (pyramids).
Smooth muscle of the urinary bladder wall; contracts during urination.
Volume of filtrate formed per minute (~125 mL/min); key measure of kidney function.
Process of moving useful substances (glucose, water, ions) from tubular filtrate back to blood.
Process of moving substances (H⁺, K⁺, drugs) from blood into tubular fluid for excretion.
Renin-Angiotensin-Aldosterone System; regulates blood pressure and fluid balance.
Released from posterior pituitary; increases water reabsorption in collecting ducts.
Intracellular fluid; ~2/3 of total body water; K⁺ is the major cation.
Extracellular fluid (~1/3 of TBW); includes plasma and interstitial fluid; Na⁺ is the major cation.
Abnormally high blood sodium; usually from water deficit → dehydration.
Low blood potassium; can cause dangerous cardiac arrhythmias.
Arterial pH < 7.35 due to HCO₃⁻ loss or acid gain; kidneys excrete H⁺, retain HCO₃⁻.
Formation of sperm from spermatogonia through meiosis; occurs in seminiferous tubules (~74 days).
Interstitial cells that produce testosterone in response to LH.
'Nurse cells' in seminiferous tubules; support, nourish, and protect developing sperm; form blood-testis barrier.
Primary male sex hormone; produced by Leydig cells; drives secondary sex characteristics, spermatogenesis, and libido.
Muscular duct that transports sperm from the epididymis to the ejaculatory duct.
Formation of oocytes; begins prenatally, arrested at prophase I; resumes at puberty with each cycle.
Release of a secondary oocyte from the ovary at mid-cycle (~day 14), triggered by LH surge.
Structure formed from the ruptured follicle post-ovulation; secretes progesterone and estrogen.
Inner lining of the uterus; thickens each cycle for implantation; sheds during menstruation.
Primary female hormone; promotes endometrial growth, secondary sex characteristics, and bone density.
Gonadotropin-releasing hormone from the hypothalamus; stimulates FSH and LH release.
Follicle-stimulating hormone; stimulates follicle growth (females) or spermatogenesis (males).
Luteinizing hormone; triggers ovulation (females) or testosterone production (males).
Hypothalamic-Pituitary-Gonadal axis; the hormonal control system for reproduction.
High estrogen in the late follicular phase triggers a positive feedback loop → LH surge → ovulation.

u9_l9.7.title: Disorders of the Urinary & Reproductive Systems

flashcards.definition: Urinary tract infection; bacterial (commonly E. coli); presents with dysuria, frequency, urgency.
Kidney stones; calcium oxalate most common; cause severe renal colic.
Chronic kidney disease; progressive nephron loss; Stage 5 (GFR <15) requires dialysis or transplant.
Treatment that filters blood when kidneys fail; hemodialysis or peritoneal dialysis.
Sexually transmitted infection; includes chlamydia, gonorrhea, syphilis, HPV, HIV, herpes.
Loop of Henle mechanism creating a medullary concentration gradient for water reabsorption.
Intrinsic renal autoregulation: afferent arteriole constricts when stretched by high BP to maintain constant GFR.
Macula densa senses NaCl in DCT; signals JGA to adjust afferent arteriole tone and renin release.
Volume of plasma completely cleared of a substance per unit time; used to estimate GFR.
Laboratory analysis of urine; screens for glucose, protein, blood, bacteria, pH, and specific gravity.
Non-specific, rapid defense present from birth; includes barriers, phagocytes, inflammation, and complement.
Specific, slower initial response; involves B cells (antibodies) and T cells; creates immunological memory.
Protein produced by plasma cells that recognizes and binds specific antigens; IgG, IgA, IgM, IgE, IgD.
Coordinates adaptive immune responses; activates B cells and CD8⁺ T cells; targeted by HIV.
Long-lived lymphocyte that enables faster, stronger immune response upon re-exposure to an antigen.
Maintenance of a stable internal environment through coordinated organ system activity.
Volume of blood pumped by the heart per minute (HR × SV); increases during exercise.
Autonomic reflex that adjusts heart rate and vascular tone in response to blood pressure changes.
Pancreatic hormone that lowers blood glucose by promoting cellular uptake and glycogen storage.
Adrenal medulla hormone that increases HR, BP, bronchodilation, and glucose — 'fight-or-flight.'
Congestive heart failure; the heart can't pump effectively → fluid backs up into lungs and tissues.
Percentage of blood ejected from the ventricle per beat; normal ~55-70%; reduced in heart failure.
Diabetic ketoacidosis; life-threatening complication of type 1 diabetes: hyperglycemia + acidosis + ketones.
Life-threatening organ dysfunction caused by a dysregulated host response to infection.
Acute respiratory distress syndrome; severe lung inflammation/fluid accumulation → respiratory failure.
Muscle contraction mechanism: actin filaments slide past myosin; powered by ATP, regulated by Ca²⁺.
Rapid electrical signal in neurons/muscles: depolarization (Na⁺ in) → repolarization (K⁺ out).
One complete heartbeat: atrial systole → ventricular systole → diastole.
Homeostatic mechanism where the response opposes the stimulus (e.g., thermoregulation).
Passive, pressure-driven movement of fluid from glomerular capillaries into Bowman's capsule.
Systematic approach: gather data → identify problems → hypothesize → test → conclude.
Recognizing that most physiological events and diseases involve interactions among multiple organ systems.
Body's automatic adjustments to maintain homeostasis when one system is stressed or failing.
Study of how disease disrupts normal physiology; connects anatomy to clinical medicine.
Process of listing and narrowing possible diagnoses based on signs, symptoms, and test results.
Culminating assignment that integrates knowledge from across all units into a comprehensive analysis.
Systematic explanation of how a disease disrupts normal physiology across organ systems.
Framework for understanding diseases by analyzing their effects on each organ system.
Using the best available research evidence to guide clinical decisions and treatments.
The signs, symptoms, and findings that a patient displays when presenting with a disease.
Study technique: retrieve information from memory rather than passively re-reading; strengthens retention.
Reviewing material at increasing intervals to improve long-term retention.
Educational framework: Remember → Understand → Apply → Analyze → Evaluate → Create; AP tests higher levels.
The central theme of anatomy/physiology; all organ systems work to maintain a stable internal environment.
Using anatomical and physiological knowledge to understand, diagnose, and treat disease.
